items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: How does myelination increase the speed of nerve impulse conduction?
items.correct: Action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction), skipping the myelinated sections.
items.misconception: Myelin makes the axon thicker so the signal travels faster through more material.
items.random_wrong: Myelin contains additional ion channels that speed up depolarisation.
items.why: Students think 'more material = faster' rather than understanding that myelin INSULATES so the impulse can leap between gaps (saltatory).

items.q: Why are frameshift mutations usually more severe than point substitutions?
items.correct: Insertions/deletions of a non-multiple of 3 shift the reading frame, changing every codon downstream of the mutation.
items.misconception: Frameshifts only change the codon at the site of the mutation, like a substitution.
items.random_wrong: Frameshift mutations are usually silent because cells can correct them.
items.why: Students see 'one base inserted' and think 'one amino acid changed', missing that the entire downstream reading frame shifts.
The answer examiners want is: Skeletal. “Muscular” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where does oxygen enter the blood?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Lungs. “Heart” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An adult takes 15 breaths per minute. How many in 4 minutes?
items.why: The real figure is 60. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
